text.tspan: 32-bit seq wraps at 4GB — timestamps distinguish old from new

div.p: Key Concepts: • 32-bit wraparound: At 10 Gbps, sequence space exhausts in ~3.4 seconds • PAWS: Uses TCP timestamp option to distinguish old from new segments • Modular arithmetic: (int32_t)(s1 - s2) < 0 handles wrap correctly • 24-day window: Maximum acceptable timestamp age for clock skew tolerance

// With PAWS: Timestamp comparison reveals order

ol.li: T=0s: Send segment A, seq=0xFFFFFFF0, ts=1000
T=0.001s: Segment A lost in network
T=3.5s: Send segment B, seq=0x00000100, ts=4500 (sequence wrapped!)
T=3.6s: Segment B arrives, accepted (ts=4500 > ts_recent)

T=4.0s: Segment A finally arrives (delayed 4s!)
seq=0xFFFFFFF0 appears "before" rcv_nxt=0x00000200
BUT ts=1000 < ts_recent=4500
PAWS rejects the stale duplicate! Without PAWS: Segment A would be accepted as "old data" and corrupt the stream with duplicate bytes.

text.tspan: // Timestamp clock must tick at least once per wrap
